record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (county-level from formation; state registration 1941)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1909 (births and deaths; compliance uneven in early years)

burned_county_authority: Wyoming has no officially designated 'burned counties.' Some early territorial records are sparse due to late settlement and minimal early government, not fires. The Wyoming State Archives in Cheyenne is the primary backup repository for county records.
administrative_notes: Wyoming Territory was organized in 1868 from parts of Dakota, Utah, and Idaho territories. Wyoming was admitted as the 44th state on July 10, 1890. It was the first territory or state to grant women full voting rights (1869). The original territorial counties were Laramie, Carter (renamed Sweetwater in 1869), Carbon, Albany, and Uinta. Wyoming has 23 counties — all formed between 1867 and 1921. The state capital has always been Cheyenne. County clerks maintain deed records, marriage records, and some vital records. District courts handle probate. The Wyoming State Archives in Cheyenne is the central repository for territorial and state government records, microfilmed county records, and historical manuscripts.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Wyoming has no burned counties. While some territorial-era records are sparse due to late and thin settlement, this reflects the frontier conditions rather than catastrophic record loss. County records generally survive from the date of county organization. The Wyoming State Archives holds microfilm copies of many county records as a preservation backup.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for Wyoming records. Houses territorial government records, governor's papers, legislative records, brand books, prison records, microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, probate), and vital records. Also holds Works Progress Administration (WPA) cemetery and interview transcripts, CCC records, and extensive photographic collections.
Publishes Annals of Wyoming and the WyoHistory.org encyclopedia. The society supports local county historical societies and facilitates access to historical records statewide.

core_repositories.source_name: American Heritage Center, University of Wyoming
core_repositories.description: One of the largest non-governmental archives in the US. Houses over 75,000 cubic feet of manuscript materials including ranching, mining, and railroad records; family papers; business records; and western history collections. Essential for Wyoming genealogy beyond county records.

Holds federal records for Wyoming including General Land Office (GLO) homestead and preemption entries, Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Wind River Reservation, federal court records (including naturalization), Bureau of Reclamation irrigation project records, and military post records for Forts Laramie, Bridger, Washakie, McKinney, and others.

familysearch_collections.description: Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, probate, court records, and vital records. Many browse-only image collections from Wyoming county clerks. Also includes indexed collections of Wyoming vital records, cemetery records, and tax rolls.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability charts, and links to digitized microfilm collections for Wyoming.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Wyoming newspapers including the Cheyenne Daily Leader (1867+), Laramie Boomerang, and county weeklies. Coverage from 1849 forward.
Wyoming Newspaper Project digital archives. Includes territorial and early statehood newspapers from communities across Wyoming.

military_records.repository: NARA / Fort Laramie National Historic Site
military_records.description: Fort Laramie (1849-1890) was the most important military post in Wyoming. Post returns, muster rolls, correspondence, and hospital records at NARA document soldiers and their families. The fort also served as an emigrant way station on the Oregon, California, and Mormon trails.

Compiled military service records for Wyoming volunteers in the Indian Wars, Spanish-American War, and World Wars. Includes Fort D.A. Russell (Cheyenne), Fort McKinney (Buffalo), Fort Washakie, and Fort Bridger records.
Wyoming State Archives holds veterans' grave registration cards and military discharge records (DD-214) filed with county clerks. WPA veterans' graves survey from the 1930s-40s.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Wyoming including homestead, preemption, desert land, and timber culture entries. Searchable database covers all federal land transactions. Wyoming was entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership.
County clerks maintain grantor/grantee deed indexes and deed books from county formation. In-person access at each county clerk's office. Some counties have digitized indexes accessible by request.

land_and_property.repository: Wyoming Livestock Board / Wyoming State Archives
land_and_property.description: Livestock brand registrations from territorial era forward. Brand books identify ranchers by name, county, and brand design. Essential for tracing ranching families. Historical brand books at the Wyoming State Archives; current records at the Wyoming Livestock Board.

vital_records.description: State registration of births and deaths from 1909. Marriage records from 1941 at state level (earlier at county). Certified copies available by mail or in person. Some early records have gaps in rural counties.

County clerks recorded births, deaths, and marriages before state registration. Coverage varies by county — some have records from territorial formation. Marriage licenses at county level are often the earliest vital records available.

church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery transcriptions compiled by DAR chapters, county historical societies, and WPA workers. Wyoming State Archives holds WPA cemetery survey records. FindAGrave has extensive Wyoming coverage. USGenWeb Tombstone Project includes some Wyoming counties.

Mormon settlement in western Wyoming (Star Valley, Afton, Evanston area) means LDS ward and branch records are significant genealogical sources. Available on FamilySearch. Also includes records of LDS emigrants who crossed Wyoming on the Mormon Trail.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Active genealogical society with published surname indexes, county histories, and cemetery transcriptions. Research library in the Laramie County Library system.

Online encyclopedia of Wyoming history with articles on counties, communities, industries, and notable figures. Provides historical context essential for understanding migration patterns and settlement history.
Free genealogy resources organized by county. Includes transcribed records, cemetery readings, family histories, and links to county-specific resources contributed by volunteers.

historical_counties_extinct.repository: Wyoming State Archives / Sweetwater County Clerk
historical_counties_extinct.description: Carter County was one of the original 1867 counties, renamed Sweetwater County in 1869. All Carter County records continued under Sweetwater County — no records were lost in the renaming. Researchers should search under both names in territorial-era documents.

historical_counties_extinct.repository: Wyoming State Archives / Johnson County Clerk
historical_counties_extinct.description: Pease County was created in 1875 and abolished in 1879, with territory absorbed into Johnson County. Very few records survive from the short-lived county. Check Johnson County and the Wyoming State Archives for any surviving Pease County records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Wind River Reservation Records — Bureau of Indian Affairs
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Wind River Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Wind River Reservation (Fremont County) is home to the Eastern Shoshone and Northern Arapaho. BIA records include census rolls, allotment records, annuity rolls, school records (Wind River Boarding School), agency correspondence, and individual Indian case files. Records at NARA Denver and the BIA Wind River Agency in Fort Washakie.

Fort Washakie served as both a military post and the BIA agency for Wind River. Agency records include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), correspondence regarding tribal members, land allotments under the Dawes Act, and school attendance records. Essential for Eastern Shoshone and Northern Arapaho genealogy.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Crow, Cheyenne, and Lakota Historical Records
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Before the reservation era, Crow, Northern Cheyenne, and Lakota (Sioux) peoples occupied portions of Wyoming. Military campaign records, treaty documents, and agency correspondence at NARA document interactions. The Dull Knife (Morning Star) outbreak of 1878-79 crossed Wyoming. Crow agency records are primarily in Montana NARA holdings.
